title: Policy evaluation and the causal analysis of public support
authors: Stefano Carattini; Robert Dur; John List

tax_mentions.context: The carbon tax British Columbia introduced in 2008 “became more popular over time” (cited evidence).
tax_mentions.notes: No author-level causal/acceptability estimation; summarizes prior study.

tax_mentions.context: BC respondents saw their carbon tax more favorably after a federal carbon tax in 2019 expanded coverage.
tax_mentions.notes: Discusses possible spillovers; relies on external evidence.

excluded_policy_mentions.reason_it_is_not_a_carbon_or_fuel_tax: Road-traffic pricing; not a tax based on carbon or fossil-fuel use.
Waste-disposal fee; targets garbage recycling incentives rather than carbon/fuel taxation.

inclusion_reason: The paper focuses on designing causal studies of public support, but it does not provide a direct acceptability/support/preferences analysis for carbon or fuel taxes.
exclusion_reason: Carbon-tax references are descriptive/cited to prior work; the paper contains no original empirical or causal acceptability analysis for carbon/fuel-tax policies.
